steps.title: Map the two halves of the sentence
steps.content: Before the blank: "an ethical AI-powered crowd-monitoring ___" — this needs a head noun (system) to complete the noun phrase. After the blank: "video streamed from cameras in heavily touristed areas, the AI algorithm determines crowd sizes..." The second part contains an independent clause ("the AI algorithm determines crowd sizes") that must be separated from what precedes it.

steps.title: Test how each punctuation choice handles the boundary
steps.content: We need (1) to close off the noun "system" and (2) to attach "Analyzing video streamed from cameras" as an introductory participial phrase to the independent clause "the AI algorithm determines crowd sizes."

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'system—analyzing': an em dash followed by "analyzing" makes the participial phrase part of the first sentence, leaving "the AI algorithm determines crowd sizes" floating as a second independent clause joined only by a comma — comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'system, analyzing': same problem — the participial phrase modifies "system," and then the independent clause is joined to it by only a comma. Comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'system. Analyzing': the period ends the first sentence cleanly; "Analyzing video streamed from cameras in heavily touristed areas," is a properly punctuated introductory participial phrase to the new independent clause. ✓
  • Choice D — 'system analyzing': no punctuation at all — the independent clause is still joined to a noun phrase by only a comma, and "system analyzing video" reads as a run-on. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
